title: Tool Boundary Drill
hook: Your agent can send email, update Jira, and call a billing API.
body: A user asks it to cancel an invoice. What should happen before the tool call?
choices: Run the billing action because the user requested it.
Confirm intent, check policy, and require a clear approval step.
Disable every external tool for all future tasks.
explanation: High-impact tools need confirmation and policy checks before execution.

title: Memory Scope Check
hook: An assistant remembers a user's cloud region preference.
body: Which memory is safest to keep long term?
choices: A reusable preference like preferred region naming style.
A copied access token from the last terminal session.
Every debug log from the user's laptop.
explanation: Durable preferences are useful; secrets and noisy logs should not become long-term memory.

title: Eval Loop Sprint
hook: Your support agent sounds good in demos but fails edge cases.
body: What gives the most reliable signal before release?
choices: A single perfect demo prompt.
A scored eval set with realistic failures and regression checks.
A prettier loading animation.
explanation: Evals turn vague quality into repeatable release gates.

title: Context Window Habit
hook: A smarter agent is not just a bigger prompt.
body: The useful habit is deciding what deserves to enter context, what can stay in memory, and what should be retrieved only when needed.
points: Keep current task state close.
Retrieve source facts late, near the decision.
Do not carry stale logs forward when the problem has changed.
reflection: Before adding more context, ask what decision the model is about to make.

title: Idempotency Reflex
hook: A payment callback may retry three times during an outage.
body: What design prevents duplicate charges?
choices: Add a retry loop inside the callback handler.
Use an idempotency key and persist the processed event result.
Trust the network to deliver the callback once.
explanation: Persisted idempotency makes retries safe and predictable.

title: Debug Signal Picker
hook: A scheduled job fails only in production.
body: Which log line is most useful?
choices: Started job.
Job failed.
Job failed with job_id, dependency, sanitized config source, and error code.
explanation: Good logs carry the state needed to separate config, code, and dependency failures.

title: API Contract Move
hook: A mobile app depends on your response field named status.
body: You need to split status into state and reason. What is safest?
choices: Remove status immediately and update docs later.
Add state and reason while keeping status through a deprecation window.
Return different fields based on the user's device.
explanation: Additive contracts reduce client breakage while allowing migration.

title: Good PR Shape
hook: A clean PR should reduce reviewer guessing.
body: The best small PRs show the behavior change, the reason, and the proof. Reviewers should not need to reverse-engineer your intent from diffs alone.
points: Keep unrelated refactors out.
Name the risk the change reduces.
Include the smallest test that proves the contract.
reflection: A good PR is not smaller because it hides work; it is smaller because it contains one coherent decision.

title: Cache Key Puzzle
hook: Two users request /report?month=May. One is admin, one is viewer.
body: What must be part of the cache key?
choices: Only the URL path.
The month, role or permission scope, and any tenant boundary.
The browser window size.
explanation: Authorization context belongs in cache keys when data visibility changes by user.

title: Race Condition Spotter
hook: Two workers read stock=1 at the same time, then both decrement.
body: What prevents selling below zero?
choices: A bigger instance.
An atomic update or transaction with a stock > 0 condition.
A faster frontend spinner.
explanation: The invariant must live where concurrent writes are serialized.

title: Binary Search Snap
hook: You need to find the first bad build among 1024 builds.
body: About how many checks do you need with binary search?
explanation: 2 to the power of 10 is 1024, so ten splits isolate the first bad build.

title: Invariant Lens
hook: Many hard bugs become smaller when you name what must never change.
body: An invariant is a rule that should survive retries, concurrency, partial failure, and messy inputs.
points: Balances never go below zero.
A paid order is fulfilled once.
A user sees only their tenant's data.
reflection: When stuck, write the invariant before writing the fix.

title: The Power of Now
hook: One useful idea: attention is a trainable skill.
body: A practical reading: notice when thought pulls you into replay or prediction, then return to the task, breath, or body signal in front of you.
points: Notice the mental loop without fighting it.
Return to one concrete signal in the present.
Then choose the next action deliberately.
reflection: Use this as a ten-second reset before deep work.
choices: Take one slow breath before the next reel.
Open three more tabs.
Ignore the body signal.
explanation: The point is not passivity; it is reducing mental noise so action becomes cleaner.

title: Deep Work
hook: One useful idea: focus is economic leverage.
body: Protect blocks where hard thinking is the default, not the exception. Shallow tasks still matter, but they should not own your best attention.
points: Put hard work where your energy is highest.
Batch shallow work instead of letting it leak everywhere.
End sessions with a visible next step.
reflection: A protected hour can beat a distracted afternoon.
choices: Block one focused work window today.
Keep notifications open during hard work.
Start with the easiest inbox item only.
explanation: The habit improves output quality because complex work needs uninterrupted context.

title: Atomic Habits
hook: One useful idea: design beats willpower.
body: Make good actions visible and easy, and make bad loops slightly harder. Tiny defaults compound when repeated.
points: Reduce friction for the habit you want.
Increase friction for the loop you regret.
Track the action, not the identity story.
reflection: Better defaults save willpower for real decisions.
choices: Move one distraction one step farther away.
Depend on motivation only.
Track nothing and hope.
explanation: Environment design reduces the number of decisions needed to do the right thing.

title: The Psychology of Money
hook: One useful idea: behavior often beats intelligence.
body: A good financial system survives fear, greed, and surprise. Room for error is not weakness; it is how plans stay alive.
points: Avoid plans that only work in perfect markets.
Keep margin for surprise.
Measure decisions by process, not one outcome.
reflection: Survival is a strategy, not a boring fallback.
choices: Leave margin before increasing risk.
Assume every forecast is certain.
Measure success only by one lucky trade.
explanation: Durable investing rewards survival and consistency, not just cleverness.

title: Trip Planner Constraint
hook: You have one day in Tokyo and want food, temples, shopping, and quiet time.
body: What is the best first planning move?
choices: Add ten neighborhoods and hope transit works.
Pick one anchor area and cluster nearby activities.
Skip meals to save time.
explanation: Clustering reduces transit drag and makes the day feel richer.

title: Travel App Design
hook: A travel app must work when the user loses data service abroad.
body: What should the app cache before the trip?
choices: Only the marketing homepage.
Reservations, maps, emergency info, and key itinerary details.
Nothing, because offline mode is rare.
explanation: Offline access protects the highest-stress moments of travel.

title: Slow Travel Rule
hook: A great trip is not measured by how many pins you visited.
body: Leave enough empty space that a cafe, side street, or local conversation can become the point of the day.
points: Choose one anchor activity.
Cluster nearby options.
Protect one unscheduled window.
reflection: The same rule works for learning: leave room to connect ideas.

title: Position Size Drill
hook: A trade idea looks strong, but earnings are tomorrow.
body: What is the healthiest action?
choices: Go all in because conviction is high.
Size the trade so being wrong does not damage the plan.
Borrow money to increase upside.
explanation: Position sizing controls downside when uncertainty rises.

title: News Reaction Filter
hook: A stock jumps after a headline. Social media is euphoric.
body: What question matters before acting?
choices: Is the headline exciting?
Does the news change cash flows, risk, or time horizon?
How many rocket comments are there?
explanation: Price action is noisy; decision quality improves when tied to fundamentals and risk.

title: Trading Journal Edge
hook: The journal is where dopamine becomes data.
body: Write the setup, invalidation point, position size, and emotional state before entering. The pattern you review later is often more valuable than the trade.
points: What would prove this idea wrong?
How much can I lose and still think clearly?
Did I follow my own rule?
reflection: A journal turns scattered wins and losses into a feedback loop.

title: Backpressure Reflex
hook: Your queue grows faster than workers can process messages.
body: What is the best first system response?
choices: Accept unlimited writes forever.
Apply rate limits, shed low-priority work, and scale workers with clear bounds.
Hide queue depth from dashboards.
explanation: Backpressure keeps overload from becoming a full system failure.

title: Circuit Breaker Move
hook: A dependency times out for 30 seconds and every request waits.
body: What pattern protects your service?
choices: Keep retrying without limits.
Use timeouts, circuit breaking, and a fallback path.
Increase the loading spinner duration.
explanation: Circuit breakers stop one dependency failure from consuming all capacity.

title: Senior PR Review
hook: A PR rewrites a module and fixes a small bug.
body: What should a strong reviewer ask first?
choices: What changed, why, what risk moved, and what tests prove it?
Can we rename every variable?
Why was this not perfect on the first try?
explanation: Good review focuses on behavior, risk, and evidence.

title: Debug Ownership
hook: Production is broken and the first theory is wrong.
body: What behavior earns trust?
choices: Keep defending the first theory.
Follow evidence across boundaries and update the theory quickly.
Wait for another team to prove everything.
explanation: Strong debugging is evidence-led and crosses ownership boundaries when needed.

title: Secret Handling
hook: A teammate suggests putting an API key in frontend JavaScript.
body: What is the safer design?
choices: Ship the key and obfuscate the variable name.
Keep the key server-side and expose only a scoped backend route.
Put the key in a comment so it is easy to find.
explanation: Browser code is inspectable; secrets belong on trusted server boundaries.

title: Feature Loop
hook: Users ask for a dashboard with twenty charts.
body: What question should shape the first version?
choices: Which decision should this dashboard make easier?
How many colors can fit on screen?
Can every metric be visible at once?
explanation: Products improve when UI starts from user decisions, not chart inventory.
